g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore weighted KNN and nearest centroid classifiers, powerful similarity-based learning methods.
Similarity-based learning classifies data based on how similar new points are to stored training instances.
Standard KNN finds the K closest neighbors and uses majority voting to predict the class of a new query point.
Weighted KNN enhances standard KNN by giving closer neighbors more influence on predictions than distant ones.
g.text: Instead of equal voting, assign weights based on distance:

g.data-narration: The weighted KNN formula uses a weighted vote: the predicted class maximizes the sum of weights for each class.
g.text: ŷ = argmax_k ( Σ wᵢ × I(yᵢ = k) )
where wᵢ = weight of i-th neighbor, I() = indicator function
Predicted class = class with highest weighted vote

g.data-narration: Four common weighting schemes: inverse distance, inverse squared distance, exponential decay, and Gaussian kernel.
The weighted KNN pipeline: calculate distances, select K neighbors, compute weights, then perform weighted voting.
This example shows weighted KNN predicting Red despite having fewer neighbors, because Red neighbors are closer.
g.text: Despite 3 Blue vs 2 Red neighbors → Predicted: RED

g.data-narration: Weighted KNN offers better accuracy but at higher computational cost compared to standard KNN.
g.text: ✓ More accurate when closer neighbors matter

g.data-narration: Weighted KNN has drawbacks: computational expense, sensitivity to weighting function choice, and curse of dimensionality.
g.text: ✗ Still suffers from curse of dimensionality

g.data-narration: Nearest Centroid Classifier represents each class by its centroid (mean vector) and classifies based on nearest centroid.
g.text: Instead of storing all points, compute the centroid (mean) of each class:
μ_c = (1/n_c) × Σᵢ xᵢ where yᵢ = c
Predict class of query point Q as: ŷ = argmin_c d(Q, μ_c)

g.data-narration: This example shows computing centroids for two classes and classifying a query point to the nearest centroid.
Nearest Centroid offers extremely fast prediction and memory efficiency but assumes spherical class distributions.
Nearest Centroid fails with complex shapes, multimodal distributions, and imbalanced classes.
Compare these algorithms: KNN uses all data, weighted KNN gives distance-based importance, nearest centroid uses class means.
g.text: Comparison: KNN vs Weighted KNN vs Nearest Centroid

g.data-narration: Feature scaling is critical for distance-based algorithms. Use min-max or z-score normalization before applying KNN or centroid methods.
g.text: Distance metrics are sensitive to feature scales. Always normalize before using distance-based classifiers:
Min-Max: x' = (x - min) / (max - min) | Z-score: x' = (x - μ) / σ

g.data-narration: Use KNN for flexibility with complex data, weighted KNN when distance matters, and nearest centroid for speed and simplicity.
g.text: • KNN: Need flexibility, complex boundaries, sufficient compute
• Weighted KNN: Closer neighbors should matter more
• Nearest Centroid: Need fast predictions, memory constrained

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Weighted KNN improves accuracy by distance-weighting, while Nearest Centroid offers speed through class prototypes.
g.text: • Weighted KNN: Gives more influence to closer neighbors → better accuracy
• Nearest Centroid: Uses class means as prototypes → extremely fast
• Both require feature scaling for distance calculations
• Choose based on: data complexity, compute budget, memory constraints

g.data-narration: Weighted KNN powers recommender systems and medical diagnosis. Nearest Centroid is used for document classification and customer segmentation.
g.text: Weighted KNN: Recommender systems, Medical diagnosis, Fraud detection
Nearest Centroid: Document classification, Customer segmentation
